speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. I see you've been started on Metformin 500mg twice daily as part of your diabetes management plan, beginning May 27th.

speaker: doctor
text: Your last diastolic blood pressure reading was 106 mmHg during that same visit.

speaker: doctor
text: You also had a fever — temperature measured at 39.1°C — which we noted at the time.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been feeling quite unwell — chills, fatigue, and increased thirst over the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: Any recent infections, urinary symptoms, or respiratory issues? Fever like this in someone with diabetes warrants careful evaluation.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs and clinical presentation, your primary diagnosis is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, confirmed on May 27th.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my blood sugar at home — it’s been running high, especially after meals.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important. Let’s review your recent glucose values — what have you been seeing?

speaker: doctor
text: In our office on May 27th, your blood glucose was 14.2 mmol/L — significantly elevated.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been trying to adjust my diet, but it’s hard when I feel so tired and nauseous.

speaker: doctor
text: Nausea and fatigue could reflect worsening hyperglycemia or an intercurrent illness — especially given your fever.

speaker: doctor
text: Just now, your point-of-care glucose came back at 16.0 mmol/L — higher than the 14.2 we saw earlier today.

speaker: patient
text: I’m worried — is this infection making my diabetes harder to control?

speaker: doctor
text: Given your persistently elevated blood pressure and new hypertension concerns, I’m going to add Amlodipine 5mg once daily, starting today.

speaker: doctor
text: And your temperature has risen further — now 39.6°C — so we’ll need to investigate for possible source of infection urgently.

speaker: patient
text: My legs have felt heavy, and my ankles are slightly swollen — could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic pressure is now 110 mmHg — up from the 106 we recorded earlier — suggesting possible acute hypertensive response to the infection.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll run blood cultures, urinalysis, and a chest X-ray to identify the cause of your fever and guide treatment.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked my blood pressure at home this morning, it was 106 mmHg — same as that reading from earlier today.

speaker: patient
text: And my fingerstick glucose just now was 14.2 mmol/L — exactly the number you mentioned from the lab draw this morning.
